name: Mixcoatl
description: Mixcoatl, whose name translates to 'Cloud Serpent' or 'Cloud Snake', is a prominent deity in Aztec mythology, associated with hunting, war, and the Milky Way. He is considered one of the four creator gods, having participated in the creation of the fifth and current sun. Mixcoatl is often depicted with a black mask, carrying hunting tools such as bows and arrows, and wearing distinctive red and white face paint. He is venerated as the patron god of the Otomi people and played a significant role in their migrations and settlements. His power extends to guiding nomadic tribes, bestowing success in hunting expeditions, and influencing the cycles of celestial events, particularly the movement of the stars across the night sky. Mixcoatl's influence is also tied to fertility and agricultural prosperity, as he is believed to ensure abundant harvests and the continuation of life. He embodies the spirit of the hunt and the warrior, representing the strength and cunning necessary for survival in a challenging world. His association with the Milky Way further connects him to the cosmos and the origins of creation. He is a complex figure whose worship encompassed a range of rituals, including human sacrifice, aimed at maintaining cosmic balance and securing his favor.
subtitle: Cloud Serpent: Hunter and War God

keyMyths.title: Creation of Fire
keyMyths.description: Mixcoatl is credited with discovering fire, crucial for human survival. He is said to have drilled into a tree with an arrow to produce the first flames, sharing this vital resource with humanity.

keyMyths.title: Patron of the Otomi
keyMyths.description: Mixcoatl is the patron deity of the Otomi people, guiding their migrations and protecting them in battle. He is central to their identity and cultural practices, ensuring their prosperity and continuity.
keyMyths.source: Otomi oral traditions and historical accounts

keyMyths.title: The Milky Way
keyMyths.description: Mixcoatl's association with the Milky Way links him to the celestial realm and the origins of the cosmos. His presence in the night sky serves as a reminder of his power and influence over the world.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
sources.work: General History of the Things of New Spain (Florentine Codex)
sources.description: A comprehensive ethnographic study of Aztec culture, including religious beliefs and practices.
sources.text: A comprehensive ethnographic study of Aztec culture, including religious beliefs and practices.
